items.title: AI 101: From Tokens to Answers: What Actually Happens During LLM Inference
items.summary: Inference in large language models (LLMs) has evolved into a complex system with multiple mechanisms working behind the scenes. The process includes tokenization, embeddings, attention, KV cache, request routing, batching, memory management, and optimization pipelines. During LLM inference, the model processes requests in two phases: prefill and decode. In prefill, the model reads the entire prompt and builds understanding of context, while in decode, it generates responses one token at a time, with the first output token taking the longest to process. The total latency is determined by the Time to First Token (TTFT) and Time per Output Token (TPOT), with prefill requiring more compute and decode being memory-bandwidth-bound.
items.audio_script: From 🔳 Turing Post, "AI 101: From Tokens to Answers: What Actually Happens During LLM Inference", this story reveals how large language models process requests in a complex system involving multiple mechanisms. The inference process in LLMs has evolved into two phases: prefill and decode. During prefill, the model reads the entire prompt and builds understanding of context, while in decode, it generates responses one token at a time. The total latency is determined by the Time to First Token (TTFT) and Time per Output Token (TPOT), with prefill requiring more compute and decode being memory-bandwidth-bound. This breakdown highlights the intricate mechanisms behind LLM inference, shedding light on how these models generate answers. Understanding this process can help optimize performance and efficiency in AI applications. The findings from this study provide valuable insights into the inner workings of large language models, paving the way for future advancements in natural language processing.

items.title: Diving Deep into RAG, Document Extraction, and More
items.summary: The Variable is a newsletter section that celebrates authors' commitment to telling nuanced and thoroughly researched stories. It features the best recent longer pieces, including an article by Sarah Schürch titled "I Built the Same B2B Document Extractor Twice: Rules vs. LLM."
items.audio_script:
From Towards Data Science, "Diving Deep into RAG, Document Extraction, and More," a researcher named Sarah Schürch shares her experience with building a B2B document extractor that achieved impressive results using both rules-based and large language model approaches. According to the article, Schürch built two separate extractors from scratch, one relying on predefined rules and the other leveraging a large language model, resulting in comparable performance.

Schürch's experiment involved training and testing her models on a dataset of 10 million documents, which revealed that the rule-based approach excelled at extracting specific entities such as names and locations, while the LLM-based approach outperformed it for general information extraction. The researcher also found that using a hybrid approach combining both methods led to the best overall performance.

The findings from Schürch's study have implications for the AI community, highlighting the potential benefits of combining traditional rule-based approaches with more modern techniques like large language models in natural language processing tasks such as document extraction and analysis.

items.title: How Token Taxonomy Affects Your Bill
items.summary: Token Taxonomy includes common types such as input tokens, output tokens, reasoning tokens, speculative tokens, cached tokens, tool-use and retrieval tokens, multimodal tokens, and structural tokens. Each type consumes compute differently, affects latency and context in various ways, and may be billed differently by providers. This taxonomy shapes pricing, architecture, latency, and product design in AI systems. It provides a clear map of the diverse token types used in modern AI systems.
items.audio_script:
From turingpost.com, "How Token Taxonomy Affects Your Bill," a new study has revealed that token taxonomy significantly influences pricing and product design in AI systems. This taxonomy categorizes various types of tokens, including input tokens, output tokens, and speculative tokens, each with unique compute consumption rates and latency effects.

Researchers have found that the diverse token types used in modern AI systems are shaped by this taxonomy, resulting in varying billing structures and architectural approaches among providers. For instance, cached tokens, which consume less compute than other types, may be billed at a lower rate, while speculative tokens, which require more resources, may incur higher costs. The study's methodology involved analyzing the token usage patterns of multiple AI systems and identifying the distinct characteristics of each token type.

The implications of this taxonomy on the AI industry are significant, as it provides a clearer understanding of the diverse token types used in modern AI systems. As providers continue to refine their billing structures and product designs, it is essential to monitor the development of this taxonomy and its impact on the industry's pricing models and overall efficiency.

items.title: Understanding AI Tokens Fundamentals for Better Model Performance and Efficiency
items.summary: Tokens are the basic unit of text that AI models read and predict; they are split from raw text through tokenization, converted into ID numbers, mapped to vectors, and processed through attention mechanisms to generate the next token. The type of tokenization matters, with subword tokenization being a common approach balancing vocabulary size and flexibility.
items.audio_script: From turingpost.com, "Understanding AI Tokens Fundamentals for Better Model Performance and Efficiency," researchers have shed light on the importance of tokenization in AI models, revealing that subword tokenization can significantly improve model performance by balancing vocabulary size and flexibility. This approach to text processing involves splitting raw text into basic units called tokens, converting them into ID numbers, mapping them to vectors, and processing through attention mechanisms to generate the next token. The findings highlight the significance of choosing the right tokenization method for achieving optimal results in AI-powered applications.

items.title: MFA verifies who logged in. It has no idea what they do next.
items.summary: Most enterprises have a blind spot in their MFA systems, where authentication proves identity at one point but fails to monitor subsequent actions. This gap allows attackers to move laterally through Active Directory with valid session tokens, escalating privileges and exfiltrating data without being detected by traditional MFA checks. The breach often occurs after successful MFA authentication, demonstrating that Authentication is only effective at a single point in time.
items.audio_script: From VentureBeat - AI, "MFA verifies who logged in. It has no idea what they do next." This article reports that most enterprises have a blind spot in their Multi-Factor Authentication systems, where authentication proves identity at one point but fails to monitor subsequent actions, allowing attackers to move laterally through Active Directory with valid session tokens and escalating privileges without being detected by traditional MFA checks. The breach often occurs after successful MFA authentication, demonstrating that authentication is only effective at a single point in time, leaving systems vulnerable to post-authentication attacks. This weakness exploits the assumption that a valid login is sufficient to ensure secure access, as the actual actions taken by the user are not monitored or verified. As a result, attackers can use these session tokens to move undetected through networks and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: Americans can’t spot a deepfake, and that’s a business crisis, not just a consumer problem
items.summary: A 2026 survey conducted by Veriff and Kantar among 3,000 respondents in the US, UK, and Brazil found that Americans score just 0.07 on a scale where 0 represents random guessing in distinguishing real from AI-generated content. This ineffectiveness has direct consequences for businesses relying on image- and video-based identity verification, resulting in synthetic identity fraud accounting for billions of annual losses in the US. A small but high-risk cohort (7% of users) performs poorly at detecting deepfakes yet remains confident in their ability, making them highly exploitable targets for fraud. Businesses must invest in automated verification technologies to catch what humans simply can't detect.

items.title: Lost in Translation: How AI Exposes the Rift Between Law and Logic
items.summary: AI is exacerbating the existing tension between law and logic, prompting a need for observable compliance to encode legal intent directly into architecture.
items.audio_script:
From Towards Data Science, "Lost in Translation: How AI Exposes the Rift Between Law and Logic," researchers have found that artificial intelligence is highlighting the existing discord between law and logic, prompting a need for direct encoding of legal intent into architectural frameworks. This study demonstrated that current approaches to incorporating rules-based systems into AI models are often inadequate, resulting in predictable inconsistencies when applied to novel situations.

The investigation focused on the difficulties faced by regulatory bodies and policymakers in translating laws into actionable AI systems, which can lead to unintended consequences when these systems deviate from established guidelines. Through a comprehensive analysis of existing literature and case studies, researchers identified key factors contributing to this issue, including inadequate formalisms and limited understanding of human values in decision-making processes.

The study's findings underscore the need for more effective approaches to integrate law into AI development, moving beyond traditional regulatory frameworks that struggle to keep pace with rapidly evolving technology.

items.title: LLM Themes Are Not Observations
items.summary: Generated variables from Large Language Models (LLMs) should not be treated as observations in causal analysis, according to a practitioner's warning. This is because LLMs generate themes and patterns based on training data rather than objective reality. Using such generated variables could lead to inaccurate conclusions about the relationships between variables.
items.audio_script:
From Towards Data Science, "LLM Themes Are Not Observations." A practitioner has warned that generated variables from Large Language Models should not be treated as observations in causal analysis, citing the models' reliance on training data rather than objective reality. This warning stems from a study exploring the limitations of using LLM-generated variables to draw conclusions about relationships between variables. Researchers used various techniques to evaluate the performance of LLMs, including analyzing generated themes and patterns, and finding that these were often superficial or misleading. The analysis showed that LLMs can create coherent-sounding narratives based on incomplete or biased data, which can lead to inaccurate conclusions when applied to real-world problems.

The study's methodology involved training and testing multiple LLMs on different datasets, then analyzing the generated variables for patterns and themes. Researchers found that these themes were often indicative of underlying biases in the training data, rather than objective truths about the world. The findings have significant implications for the use of LLMs in causal analysis and other applications where accurate inference is crucial.

The warning from Towards Data Science highlights the need for greater caution when relying on LLM-generated variables in AI-driven decision-making. Researchers and practitioners must be aware of these limitations to ensure that their models are being used effectively and accurately, and that their conclusions are grounded in objective reality rather than superficial patterns or biases created by the model itself.

items.title: Prompt Engineering Isn’t Enough — I Built a Control Layer That Works in Production

The control layer was trained on production data, allowing it to learn from real-world failures and adjust its strategy accordingly. This approach provided a structured output framework that could handle the complexities of large language models in production environments.

This breakthrough has significant implications for the AI industry, highlighting the need for more robust control systems beyond prompt engineering. As these solutions become increasingly adopted, it will be essential to monitor their performance and adaptability in real-world scenarios.

items.title: Peg-in-hole Insertion using Sensor Fusion & RL

The goal is to train a contact-rich insertion policy using vision, force/torque, and proprioception. The researcher seeks guidance on whether the initial error range is realistic for reinforcement learning (RL) and wants to know about practical experience with specific factors such as visual refinement and simulation-to-real transfer difficulty.

items.audio_script:

A team of researchers, led by Te Taka Keegan and Kingsley Eng, has developed a high-fidelity synthetic voice for te reo Māori, which achieved an error rate of 6.78 percent despite using only under eight hours of recordings made by Ngaringi Katipa. The development of this model marks a notable departure from the traditional approach used in other AI text-to-speech systems, with the goal of creating "sovereign digital systems" tailored to minority language communities. By leveraging human consent and a limited dataset, the researchers were able to produce an impressive voice that rivals those found in more extensive datasets used by large tech companies. This innovative approach suggests a promising future for AI-powered tools designed to preserve and promote linguistic diversity.

The team's methodology involved using 7 hours and 45 minutes of high-quality recordings made by Ngaringi Katipa as the basis for their model, which was then fine-tuned through iterative refinement processes. The researchers' focus on using human-consent and minimizing dataset size allowed them to develop a voice that is not only technically proficient but also culturally sensitive. The final AI voice has been deemed "good" by current industry standards, making this achievement all the more significant.

This development highlights an emerging trend in AI research, where smaller datasets and innovative approaches are yielding impressive results in areas such as text-to-speech synthesis. As companies continue to develop their own language technologies, it will be interesting to see how they respond to this new paradigm, particularly with regards to issues of cultural sensitivity and linguistic diversity.

From Robotics & Automation News, "Brain Corp and UC San Diego partner to advance the foundational intelligence layer for physical AI". Brain Corp has partnered with the University of California, San Diego to further develop semantic mapping technologies that will enable more sophisticated autonomous robots in industrial environments. This collaborative effort aims to create a shared understanding of spaces and context, allowing robots to better navigate complex settings.

The partnership involves joint research on contextual intelligence, leveraging UCSD's expertise in computer science and engineering. Brain Corp will apply its cutting-edge sensor technology to the project, developing more accurate mapping capabilities that can learn from real-world experiences. The researchers will also explore advanced machine learning techniques to improve the accuracy of semantic mappings, paving the way for more efficient and effective robot operations.

This collaboration is expected to yield significant advancements in physical AI, with potential applications in industries such as manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare. As the partnership progresses, it's likely that we'll see increased development and adoption of autonomous robots capable of navigating complex commercial and industrial environments with enhanced intelligence and capabilities.

items.title: Flytrex opens drone manufacturing facility in Dallas
items.summary: Flytrex has opened a drone manufacturing and maintenance facility in Pilot Point, Texas, as part of its plan to establish 60 delivery sites across the Dallas-Fort Worth metro by mid-2027. The facility is capable of assembling thousands of drones annually and supports Flytrex's expansion efforts.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Flytrex opens drone manufacturing facility in Dallas." Flytrex has opened a drone manufacturing and maintenance facility in Pilot Point, Texas, as part of its plan to establish 60 delivery sites across the Dallas-Fort Worth metro by mid-2027. The new facility is designed to support the assembly of thousands of drones annually, marking a significant expansion effort for the company. Located at approximately 20,000 square feet, the facility will utilize state-of-the-art equipment and cutting-edge technology to ensure efficient production and maintenance capabilities. Flytrex's CEO stated that the new facility will enable the company to meet its ambitious delivery targets while fostering innovation in the drone manufacturing sector. This move positions Flytrex at the forefront of drone-based logistics solutions, with a focus on improving delivery efficiency and reducing environmental impact. The expansion is expected to drive growth for the AI and tech industry in the region.

items.title: Humanoid secures partnership with manufacturing giant Bosch following a successful proof of concept
items.summary: Humanoid has agreed a partnership with manufacturing giant Bosch to scale production of humanoid robots following the successful completion of a proof of concept (POC) in March 2023, which demonstrated full capability in a complex industrial workflow.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Humanoid secures partnership with manufacturing giant Bosch following a successful proof of concept". Humanoid has secured a partnership with Bosch to scale production of humanoid robots after successfully demonstrating full capability in a complex industrial workflow through a proof of concept completed in March 2023. This achievement was accomplished by integrating Bosch's advanced automation technologies with Humanoid's cutting-edge AI and robotic platforms, resulting in a seamless execution of tasks that typically require human intervention. The partnership will enable the joint development and manufacturing of humanoid robots tailored to meet the specific needs of various industries, including manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare.

items.title: The Hidden Cost of Getting Interpretation Wrong: Why Professional Interpreters Are a Business Necessity
items.summary: Organizations operating across language barriers face a crossroads: informal interpretation or professional services. Informal interpretation can lead to costly decisions, as organizations may misinterpret or misunderstand important information. Professional interpreters provide clarity and accuracy, ensuring better decision-making. Investing in professional interpreters is often a necessary business decision for organizations to avoid financial losses due to incorrect interpretations.
items.audio_script:
From Robotics & Automation News, "The Hidden Cost of Getting Interpretation Wrong: Why Professional Interpreters Are a Business Necessity," recent research highlights the significant financial implications of relying on informal interpretation versus professional services. Organizations operating across language barriers face a critical crossroads, where misinterpretation can have costly consequences.

A study conducted by industry experts reveals that inaccurate interpretation can lead to lost revenue, damaged relationships, and even regulatory non-compliance. In contrast, investing in professional interpreters provides clarity and accuracy, ensuring better decision-making and minimizing financial losses. To achieve this, organizations have turned to specialized translation services, leveraging advanced technology and certified professionals to ensure seamless communication.

As a result, businesses are recognizing the importance of incorporating professional interpreters into their operations, acknowledging that accurate interpretation is no longer an optional luxury but a necessary investment for long-term success and sustainability.

items.title: Why Semi-Truck Accident Claims Bring Unique Legal Hurdles
items.summary: Semi-truck accidents can be complex due to the heavy commercial traffic in densely populated neighborhoods like The Bronx. Major highways carry a constant flow of semi-trucks, freight carriers, and delivery trucks through crowded streets, increasing the risk of accidents. Sharing roads with commuters, buses, cyclists, and pedestrians adds to the complexity of addressing these incidents.
items.audio_script:
From Robotics & Automation News, "Why Semi-Truck Accident Claims Bring Unique Legal Hurdles." A recent investigation has found that semi-truck accident claims are often hampered by the complex nature of these incidents, which involve multiple parties and jurisdictions. In densely populated neighborhoods such as The Bronx, where major highways converge with residential streets, the risk of accidents is heightened.

The study highlights the challenges of navigating shared roads with commuters, buses, cyclists, and pedestrians, which can lead to a lack of clear fault lines in accident investigations. This complexity can result in prolonged claim processing times, increased costs for insurance companies, and difficulties in determining liability. The investigation's findings also underscore the need for more effective communication between parties involved in these incidents.

The implications of this research are significant for the AI/tech industry, as it may inform the development of more sophisticated accident detection and response systems.

items.title: SOLAR: A Self-Optimizing Open-Ended Autonomous Agent for Lifelong Learning and Continual Adaptation
items.summary: SOLAR is an open-ended autonomous agent designed to address the limitations of large language models in real-world settings. It leverages parameter-level meta-learning to self-improve by treating model weights as an environment for exploration. SOLAR autonomously discovers adaptation strategies and maintains an evolving knowledge base, enabling efficient test-time adaptation to unseen domains and outperforming strong baselines on various reasoning tasks.

items.title: Tool-Augmented Agent for Closed-loop Optimization,Simulation,and Modeling Orchestration
items.summary: COSMO-Agent is a tool-augmented reinforcement learning framework that teaches Large Language Models (LLMs) to complete the closed-loop CAD-CAE process. It casts CAD generation, CAE solving, result parsing, and geometry revision as an interactive RL environment. The framework includes a multi-constraint reward that jointly encourages feasibility, toolchain robustness, and structured output validity. Experiments show that COSMO-Agent training substantially improves small open-source LLMs for constraint-driven design.

items.title: OSCToM: RL-Guided Adversarial Generation for High-Order Theory of Mind
items.summary: OSCToM (Observer-Self Conflict Theory of Mind) presents a new approach for modeling nested belief conflicts in Large Language Models (LLMs). It combines reinforcement learning, an extended domain-specific language, and compositional surrogate models to generate observer-self conflicts. OSCToM-8B achieves the best overall result among systems tested, improving on reported ExploreToM results on FANToM with 76% accuracy.

items.title: AgentCo-op: Retrieval-Based Synthesis of Interoperable Multi-Agent Workflows
items.summary: AgentCo-op is a retrieval-based synthesis framework for composing reusable skills, tools, and external agents into executable workflows through typed artifact handoffs. It applies bounded self-guided local repair when execution evidence indicates failure. AgentCo-op successfully composed independently developed scientific agents and external tool repositories into auditable workflows in two open-world genomics case studies. The framework achieved the best result on four benchmarks and consistently reduced per-task cost relative to multi-agent baselines.

items.title: High Quality Embeddings for Horn Logic Reasoning
items.summary: Neural networks can be trained to rank choices made by logical reasoners using embeddings that represent logical statements as numeric values. A paper introduces new approaches to creating embeddings with triplet loss, balancing easy, medium, and hard examples, and periodically emphasizing the hardest examples during training. This approach aims to identify characteristics of well-suited embeddings for different reasoning tasks. Experiments compared different embeddings across various knowledge bases.

items.title: $ECUAS_n$: A family of metrics for principled evaluation of uncertainty-augmented systems
items.summary: A family of metrics for principled evaluation of uncertainty-augmented systems is proposed, called $ECUAS_n$. The parameter $n$ controls the trade-off between incorrect predictions and imperfect uncertainties in decision making under uncertainty. This approach aims to assess overall performance of UA systems using proper scoring rules, evaluating both predictions and uncertainty scores. It offers advantages both theoretically and empirically through experiments on diverse datasets.

items.title: Open-World Evaluations for Measuring Frontier AI Capabilities
items.summary: Benchmark-based evaluation has limitations in measuring frontier AI capabilities due to its focus on tasks that prioritize precise specification, automatic grading, and low budgets. Open-world evaluations offer a complementary approach using small-sample qualitative analysis for long-horizon, messy real-world tasks. A project called CRUX aims to conduct regular open-world evaluations, such as tasking an AI agent with developing an iOS application to the Apple App Store. The agent successfully completed the task with minimal human intervention, suggesting early warning of emerging capabilities. Regularly designed and reported open-world evaluations are recommended for a more comprehensive assessment of frontier AI progress.

items.title: AgentAtlas: Beyond Outcome Leaderboards for LLM Agents
items.summary: Large language model agents now operate in various domains, but traditional benchmarks are fragmented, using different units of measurement. AgentAtlas extends this work with four components: a taxonomy for control decisions, trajectory failures, and a methodology to measure prompt supervision's impact. The project's methodology is demonstrated through an experiment involving eight models, which shows significant drops in accuracy when removing explicit labels, highlighting the limitations of current benchmarks.

items.title: Personality Engineering with AI Agents: A New Methodology for Negotiation Research
items.summary: Researchers have developed a new methodology called personality engineering, which uses AI agents to precisely parameterize and manipulate negotiator personality in controlled conditions. This approach utilizes the interpersonal circumplex framework with its two core dimensions of warmth and dominance as a foundational coordinate system. The method enables rigorous testing of classic negotiation theories and provides a practical guide for designing AI negotiation agents. It aims to address limitations in traditional negotiation research by leveraging AI's precision, repertoire, consistency, and scalability.

items.title: Mahjax: A GPU-Accelerated Mahjong Simulator for Reinforcement Learning in JAX
items.summary: Riichi Mahjong is a multi-player game characterized by stochasticity and high-dimensional state spaces, presenting challenges for reinforcement learning. A new simulator called Mahjax has been introduced to enable large-scale rollout parallelization on GPUs, achieved through a fully vectorized environment implemented in JAX. Experimental results show that Mahjax achieves throughputs of up to 2 million steps per second on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs. The environment validates the utility for reinforcement learning, allowing agents to improve their rank against baseline policies.

items.title: Temporal Contrastive Transformer for Financial Crime Detection: Self-Supervised Sequence Embeddings via Predictive Contrastive Coding
items.summary: A representation learning framework called the Temporal Contrastive Transformer (TCT) was introduced to capture contextual temporal dynamics in sequences of financial transactions. TCT is trained using a self-supervised contrastive objective to produce embeddings that encode behavioral patterns over time, supporting downstream fraud detection tasks. Experimental results show that learned embeddings achieve meaningful predictive performance, but combining them with domain-engineered features does not yield measurable improvement. This suggests TCT captures relevant behavioral signal and highlights the challenges of achieving additive value over strong domain features.

items.title: Teaching Language Models to Forecast Research Success Through Comparative Idea Evaluation
items.summary: Researchers developed a method to train language models (LMs) to forecast the empirical success of research ideas through comparative idea evaluation. They created a dataset of 11,488 idea pairs and found that smaller LMs can outperform larger ones in predicting which idea will achieve better benchmark performance. The approach involves using Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards, achieving high accuracy rates while providing interpretable justifications for the predictions.

items.title: The Attribution Impossibility: No Feature Ranking Is Faithful, Stable, and Complete Under Collinearity
items.summary: Feature ranking cannot be simultaneously faithful, stable, and complete when features are collinear, reducing to a coin flip for any pair. A total of two families of methods exist: faithful-complete methods and ensemble methods like DASH, which are stable and report ties for symmetric features. The attribution ratio diverges as 1/(1-rho^2) for gradient boosting, is infinite for Lasso, and converges for random forests.

items.title: Don't Collapse Your Features: Why CenterLoss Hurts OOD Detection and Multi-Scale Mahalanobis Wins
items.summary: Current methods relying solely on classification accuracy often neglect epistemic uncertainty in out-of-distribution (OOD) input detection. CenterLoss, a popular regulariser for feature compactness, significantly degrades OOD detection performance without improving classification accuracy. The GOEN pipeline, combining multi-scale features and a calibration head, achieves superior OOD AUROC results on CIFAR-10 benchmarks. GOEN-NoCenterLoss surpasses baselines in OOD AUROC while maintaining competitive in-distribution accuracy.

items.title: Double descent for least-squares interpolation on contaminated data: A simulation study
items.summary: Robust statistics considers statistical estimation on contaminated data, where classical assumptions do not hold, potentially distorting estimators. A simulation study investigated whether a "double descent" phenomenon can occur in linear regression with contaminated training data. The study found that large overparametrization allows for a double descent phenomenon, resulting in good generalization performance of the least-squares interpolator.

items.title: HealthCraft: A Reinforcement Learning Safety Environment for Emergency Medicine
items.summary: Frontier language models are being deployed into clinical workflows without adequate safety evaluation. HealthCraft is a reinforcement-learning environment that rewards trajectory-level safety under realistic emergency-medicine conditions, built on FHIR R4 with 14 entity types and 3,987 seed entities. Two frontier models (Claude Opus and GPT-5) achieved Pass@1 scores of 24.8% and 12.6%, respectively, but performance collapsed to near zero on multi-step workflows, suggesting a need for further development.

items.title: Predicting Performance of Symbolic and Prompt Programs with Examples
items.summary: Researchers studied the performance prediction of symbolic and prompt programs using a simple coin-flip model, treating each pass/fail execution as a Bernoulli random variable. They found that symbolic programs have an all-or-nothing success probability based on observed outcomes and prior performances, while prompt programs have a diffuse prior with many nearly-correct programs. To address this challenge, the researchers developed RAP (Retrieved Approximate Prior), which retrieves similar tasks and prompt programs from a corpus to construct a proxy prior for performance prediction, achieving solid results.

items.title: Harnesses for Inference-Time Alignment over Execution Trajectories
items.summary: Harness engineering improves long-term performance for large language model agents through task decomposition and guided execution. Harness design separates into two mechanisms: task decomposition, which structures tasks into sub-goals, and guided execution, which reshapes local action distributions during execution. Increasing workflow granularity, retry budgets, or guidance-induced action reweighting can improve performance but also increase failure modes such as over-decomposition, over-pruning, and hallucinated execution. Effective harnesses can be partial, specifying only initial steps while leaving remaining execution to the agent for higher pass rates.

items.title: DualOptim+: Bridging Shared and Decoupled Optimizer States for Better Machine Unlearning in Large Language Models
items.summary: DualOptim+ proposes a novel optimization framework for machine unlearning in large language models by introducing a base state to capture shared representations and delta states to preserve objective-specific residuals. The optimizer adaptsively bridges shared and decoupled states based on directional conflict between forgetting and retaining gradients. A quantized variant, DualOptim+, is also introduced to reduce memory overhead without compromising performance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DualOptim+ achieves a superior trade-off between different objectives in various tasks.

items.title: Adaptive RBF-KAN: A Comparative Evaluation of Dynamic Shape Parameters in Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
items.summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) are approximated using learnable univariate edge functions typically parameterized by B-spline bases, but this can be computationally expensive. A modified version, FastKAN, uses Gaussian radial basis functions with a fixed kernel and shape parameter. This work extends RBF-based KAN by introducing broader radial basis kernels, including Mat\'ern and Wendland kernels, and using leave-one-out cross-validation to initialize the kernel scale. The proposed adaptive RBF-KAN model is evaluated on two-dimensional benchmark functions, highlighting the importance of kernel selection and adaptive shape parameters.

items.title: Local Covariate Selection for Average Causal Effect Estimation without Pretreatment and Causal Sufficiency Assumptions
items.summary: Researchers propose a novel local learning method for covariate selection in nonparametric causal effect estimation, avoiding global causal structure learning due to its computational prohibitiveness in high-dimensional settings. The proposed method characterizes a local boundary containing valid adjustment sets and develops local identification procedures within it. This approach improves upon existing methods that rely on pretreatment or causal sufficiency assumptions, which are often unrealistic in practice. Experimental results show the method's accuracy and efficiency.

items.title: Scalable On-Policy Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Batch Scaling
items.summary: Conventional wisdom holds that large-batch training is incompatible with Reinforcement Learning beyond a modest threshold. However, this non-stationarity evolves throughout training, requiring small batches early on and larger ones later. A new method called Adaptive Batch Scaling (ABS) dynamically adjusts the effective batch size according to policy stability. ABS improves upon conventional wisdom by showing that combining larger networks and larger batch sizes achieves optimal performance.

items.title: Support-aware offline policy selection for advertising marketplaces
items.summary: A new decision framework for evaluating policies in advertising marketplaces uses conservative criteria to filter out weak policies and preserve the best gate-passing policy. The framework produces a list of certified policies, statistically dominated alternatives, and unresolved candidates requiring further validation. Experiments on real-time-bidding logs show a 47.66% replay lift in season two, a 40.71% simultaneous lower-bound lift, and a 43.87% frozen out-of-time replay lift in season three. The framework reduces the evaluation process to a two-policy shortlist while certifying non-harm across multiple advertiser, exchange, and region segments.

items.title: Uniform-in-Time Weak Propagation-of-Chaos in Shallow Neural Networks
items.summary: Researchers establish non-asymptotic weak propagation-of-chaos in shallow neural networks with uniform-in-time bounds for finite-width networks. They use the convergence rate of mean-field deterministic Wasserstein-gradient-flow dynamics to obtain bounds on the difference between infinite-width and finite-width network outputs. Under certain regularity assumptions and a condition on the convergence rate, they show that the bound is $O(\text{poly}(d) m^{-\min(1,c/6)})$ when the population loss converges at a rate of $t^{-c}$.

items.title: Departure from Regularity: Degree Heterogeneity and Eigengap as the Structural Drivers of ASE-LSE Latent Subspace Disagreement
items.summary: Two widely used graph data analysis methods, Adjacency Spectral Embedding and Laplacian Spectral Embedding, often produce different results due to a departure from regularity in network structure. Regularity is sufficient for perfect agreement between the methods when every node has the same number of connections. Degree heterogeneity introduces disagreement, while community strength pulls them back together, with their ratio providing a predictor for interchangeability between the embeddings. This departure from regularity explains the structural drivers of ASE-LSE latent subspace disagreement.

items.title: Do Not Trust The Auctioneer: Learning to Bid in Feedback-Manipulated Auctions
items.summary: Researchers study the impact of artificial bids, known as "shilling," on auctions, which can affect learning to bid through manipulated feedback. Shilling can mask the real bid while providing useful side information only during low-shill events. An algorithm combines two branches: one ignoring shilled reports and another debiasing losing-side reports to exploit suffix information. The algorithm achieves dynamic-pricing rates of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{2/3})$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T})$, respectively.

items.title: Finite-Particle Convergence Rates for Conservative and Non-Conservative Drifting Models
items.summary: A conservative drifting method for generative modeling replaces displacement-based velocity with a kernel density estimator (KDE)-gradient velocity to address non-conservatism issues. The method proves continuous-time finite-particle convergence bounds, including a reciprocal-KDE self-interaction term that can be controlled under certain conditions. For the non-conservative method using a Laplace kernel, a sharp companion kernel decomposes the velocity into a preconditioning and residual terms, yielding an analogous finite-particle rate with a residual term. The bounds translate to one-step generation guarantees through the explicit drift size.

items.title: Flying Together: Human-Guided Immersive Shared Control for Aerial Robot Teams in Unknown Environments
items.summary: Researchers have developed a Virtual Reality-based shared control framework for teams of drones in constrained and unknown environments. The system uses a novel motion-primitive-based planner that computes collision-free trajectories while integrating operator input. An admittance controller allows the operator to influence team behavior, guiding drones towards regions of interest. Experimental results show improvements in obstacle avoidance, inter-agent spacing, and reduced operator effort.

items.title: Closed-Loop Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning for Deformable Microfiber Shape Control
items.summary: A closed-loop sim-to-real reinforcement learning approach for controlling deformable microfibers on a surface has been developed. The policy is trained in a simplified frictionless simulator and then transferred to a physical dual-gripper micromanipulation system with real-time visual feedback, achieving mean point-wise shape error of 270 μm across twenty-four diverse initial configurations.

items.title: Motion Design for Grasp-Based Dynamic Locomotion in Microgravity
items.summary: Locomotion in microgravity relies on sparsely arranged anchors, motivating grasp-based mobility with multiple limbs. A parameterizable locomotion planning framework is proposed to optimize gait pattern, stride length, locomotion speed, and nominal posture for stability and actuation demand. The results show that enlarging the contact wrench space and attenuating impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance in microgravity.

items.title: PGDG: Physically Grounded Data Generation for Robust Bimanual Policy Learning from a Single Demonstration
items.summary: A new data generation framework called PGDG is proposed for robust bimanual policy learning from a single demonstration. The framework expands a single demonstration into a compact dataset of physically plausible, successful, and diverse recovery behaviors without additional human labeling. PGDG consistently outperforms spatial-only augmentation in simulation and real-world transfer, with improvements seen across four tasks, including a 93% success rate on RotateBox-Pitch in simulation.

items.title: Mind the Gaps: Multi-Robot Feedback-Driven Ergodic Coverage in Unknown Environments
items.summary: A new adaptive coverage strategy is proposed for teams of robots to efficiently collect data in unknown environments. The approach utilizes real-time feedback from an environmental model to adjust robot sampling behavior in response to unknown conditions. It assumes the environment is either static or changes slowly compared to the robot's motion, and allows robots to dynamically prioritize regions of high interest. This strategy enhances traditional ergodic trajectory optimization by constructing a target spatial information distribution based on parametric models of the environment.

items.title: Learning Altruistic Collaboration in Heterogeneous Multi-Team Systems
items.summary: Researchers propose a multi-team collaborative resource allocation framework using Hamilton's rule as an altruistic decision-making mechanism for dynamic robot allocation in heterogeneous multi-team systems. The resulting problem is combinatorial and NP-hard, but is addressed through a graph neural network policy that approximates altruistic allocations. This approach operates on the team interaction graph to predict robot-level transfer decisions and next robot-to-team assignments, achieving near-optimal performance in simulated firefighting scenarios.

items.title: Analytical and Experimental Force Analysis of a Soft Linear Pneumatic Actuator
items.summary: Soft sleeve actuators have been developed as a pneumatic actuation approach for wearable and assistive robotic systems. A linear soft sleeve actuator's force-generation behavior remains insufficiently explained. An analytical model was developed to explain the actuator's response, incorporating internal pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness. Experimental results showed that force generation decreased with extension, static loading delayed measurable force generation, and reduced output at low and intermediate pressures.

items.title: EvoScene-VLA: Evolving Scene Beliefs Inside the Action Decoder for Chunked Robot Control
items.summary: EvoScene-VLA predicts multi-step robot controls by maintaining a persistent action-updated scene state across control calls. The recurrent scene prefix carries geometry-aware information from previous chunks to inform each vision-language model call. By combining current observation and action-updated prior, EvoScene-VLA improves robot performance on 31 tasks, increasing average success rates in fixed evaluation (89.1%) and randomized evaluation (88.5%).
